world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lui character portrait appears in Japanese bad ending; absent in US version
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: narrative compression in US version (NPCs removed, simplified endings) reflects localization for unfamiliar audience with minimal Hokuto no Ken exposure

environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese version places NPCs at stage conclusions, communicating that Kenshiro's journey touches allied or civilian populations; US version removes these to streamline narrative
Japanese version implements random side-screen projectiles mirroring previous game; creates visual sense of ambush danger and environmental hostility
Japanese version requires head strikes on early mini-bosses, establishing martial-arts-specific combat storytelling (Hokuto techniques target pressure points)
Kenshiro's blue (US) vs. black (JP) palette change signals regional adaptation; blue makes protagonist more visually distinct in NES color palette constraints
Japanese kanji 'abeshi' and 'hidebu' (death cries from manga) replaced with generic white/black stars in US; removes cultural specificity but maintains mechanical clarity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: navigates stages left-to-right platformer style; responds to enemy encounters with melee combat
requires targeted head strikes to defeat; implies specific martial-technique vulnerability matching Hokuto no Ken franchise mechanics
standard hit-anywhere defeat pattern; simplified for US audience unfamiliar with series lore
throw projectiles randomly from screen sides, creating environmental ambush threat
standard melee engagement only; projectile threat removed, lowering difficulty
player dropped at Stage 4 regardless of current position; suggests mandatory difficulty reset or narrative checkpoint
player continues from current stage; enables player-paced progression

notes: Source material (TCRF) provides no direct visual asset descriptions, color palettes, or detailed environmental design. Extraction derives from regional difference documentation, which implies visual/behavioral communication patterns. Japanese version emphasizes franchise-specific martial-arts storytelling (head strikes, kanji power-ups, NPC faction markers); US version simplifies for unfamiliar audience. Protagonist color change (black→blue) suggests NES palette optimization. Power-up symbol translation ('abeshi'/'hidebu'→white/black stars) represents cultural localization strategy. Continue mechanic difference (Stage 4 drop vs. current stage) communicates world-state preservation philosophy shift. Lack of explicit danger/faction visual cues in source suggests these systems are communicated through gameplay behavior and level design rather than symbolic UI.
